title: What Does a 6-Hour Snapshot Constrain?
description:
## Mapping a Backup Schedule to the Right Metric

An application's only backup mechanism is an automated **snapshot of its database taken every 6 hours**. There is no continuous replication or transaction-log shipping. During a DR review, an architect is asked: given this 6-hour snapshot interval (and assuming restore time itself is handled separately), which recovery metric does this backup schedule directly determine, and what is its worst-case value?

options.text: It determines the RTO, which is up to 6 hours of downtime
It determines the RPO, which is up to 6 hours of potential data loss
It determines both RTO and RPO equally at 6 hours
It determines neither; snapshot frequency is unrelated to RTO and RPO

explanation: **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** is the maximum acceptable amount of **data loss** measured as a time window — it is governed by how often you capture recoverable copies of the data. With snapshots every 6 hours and nothing in between, a failure just before the next snapshot loses up to ~6 hours of changes, so the snapshot interval **directly sets the RPO at up to 6 hours**. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** is the maximum acceptable **downtime to recover**, which is driven by how long it takes to detect, provision, and restore — not by how often you snapshot — so A and C are wrong (they conflate the two metrics). D is wrong because backup frequency is precisely what bounds RPO. To reduce RPO below 6 hours you would snapshot more frequently or add continuous replication / log shipping.

hints.title: Two different questions
hints.body: RPO answers "how much data can I lose?" (data-loss window). RTO answers "how long until I'm back up?" (downtime). Which one does backup frequency bound?

hints.title: Where the 6 hours lives
hints.body: The gap between backups is the window of changes you could lose — that's the data-loss metric, not the recovery-time metric.
